zotero.abstractNote: How do children and adolescents learn to explore their environment? While previous research has focused on age-related changes in exploration strategies, the underlying computational mechanisms remain poorly understood. Here we show that developmental changes in exploration can be characterized as a process of stochastic optimization. We tested 155 participants aged 8–25 years in a multi-armed bandit task and found that exploration becomes more directed and efficient with age. Using a computational model of stochastic optimization, we demonstrate that these developmental changes are best explained by a reduction in the noise of the optimization process, rather than a shift in the exploration strategy itself. Our results suggest that the development of exploration is driven by an increasing ability to effectively navigate the trade-off between exploration and exploitation, providing a new framework for understanding cognitive development.
